qa_pairs.question: What should users consider when employing the `-O3` optimization option with the GCC compiler?
qa_pairs.answer: The GCC compiler's `-O3` option includes optimizations that may be unsafe for certain types of code, such as those relying on aliasing. If there is any uncertainty, it is recommended to compile and optimize code using the `-O2` option instead. Users with more time can consult the GCC man page (e.g., `man g++`) to identify and selectively disable unsafe options included within `-O3`.

qa_pairs.question: What issues can arise when linking C++ code due to Application Binary Interface (ABI) changes in GCC, and how can these issues be resolved?
qa_pairs.answer: Major changes to the GCC Application Binary Interface (ABI), such as the one between GCC version 4.9 and 5.1, can cause linking to fail if different compiler versions are used for the source code and its dependent libraries, especially with precompiled vendor libraries. To resolve this, it's generally best to either recompile older binaries from source with the newer compiler or to maintain consistency by using the same compiler version. For GCC version 5.1 or higher, the Dual ABI feature can be utilized by passing options like `-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0` to link with libraries built using an older ABI. GCC also provides the `-fabi-version` option for explicit control over ABI compatibility.

qa_pairs.question: How can users of Intel C/C++ compilers ensure ANSI/ISO/IEEE standards-compliant floating-point support?
qa_pairs.answer: Intel C/C++ compilers may default to using floating-point optimizations that could be unsafe. To ensure ANSI/ISO/IEEE standards-compliant floating-point support, users are advised to read the Intel man pages (e.g., `man icpc`) and use one of two specific options: `-fp-model precise` or `-fp-model source`. Further details are available in the Intel slideshow titled "Floating-point control in the Intel compiler and libraries".
